name: New Jersey
regulatoryOwner: New Jersey Department of Environmental Protection
delegatedLocalAuthorities: NJDEP guidance and certified closure contractors matter fast in New Jersey, especially when the seller cannot produce clean closure paperwork.
buyerSellerSummary: In New Jersey, a buried-tank question during a sale usually turns on seller disclosure, closure proof, and whether the tank is still actually there.
recordLookupSummary: Start with the seller disclosure, any tank closure permit or invoice, and oil-to-gas paperwork before you treat silence as proof.
sweepFirstSummary: A sweep is often the right first move when old fill or vent pipes remain and no one can prove the tank was handled correctly.
removalVsAbandonmentSummary: Closure choices depend on confirmed tank conditions, site access, and the NJDEP closure path tied to the property.
leakReportingSummary: If anyone mentions a release, a fund claim, or a no-further-action letter, the issue stops being simple closure and starts becoming cleanup review.
insuranceCleanupSummary: Cleanup and reimbursement questions often run on a different track from a simple locate or closure job.
quickAnswer: In New Jersey, get the disclosure, closure paperwork, and tank evidence straight before you talk credits, removal, or cleanup.
commonTriggers: Old fill or vent pipes show up before closing.
The seller cannot produce a closure permit, invoice, or clean disclosure.
Inspection notes raise the buried-tank issue without confirming whether the tank is still there.
cautions: Do not assume missing paperwork proves there is no tank.
Do not blur ordinary closure with spill or remediation questions.
Do not jump to hard cost talk before the paperwork or sweep result is in hand.
strongestTriggerStates: The buyer or seller has no clean closure document, but old oil-heat evidence is still visible on site.
An inspection note or sweep result suggests a tank while the sale timeline is still live.
Someone mentions an NJDEP case, fund claim, or cleanup history before the facts are sorted.
firstMoves: Ask for the seller disclosure form, any closure permit or contractor invoice, and oil-to-gas paperwork in one request.
If old fill or vent pipes remain, order a sweep before you let the issue turn into a removal quote.
If anyone mentions an NJDEP case number, fund claim, or no-further-action letter, switch quickly into cleanup facts.
documentTargets: Seller disclosure form, tank closure permit or contractor invoice, and oil-to-gas conversion paperwork.
Any NJDEP case number, fund paperwork, or no-further-action letter tied to the property.
Removal invoices, transfer documents, or inspection records explaining when the system changed.
escalationSignals: Odor, staining, excavation history, or paperwork showing this is more than a missing-record problem.
Any NJDEP document that references a release, remediation, or no-further-action decision.
A contractor or inspection note confirming tank presence and raising contamination concern.
